validated_rows.control_id: Establish normative legal framework ensuring ethical and responsible AI use within value-based principles
Develop ethical guidelines for AI use protecting individual rights and mitigating negative societal impacts
Ensure fair distribution of AI use and benefits; protect individual rights
Ensure data privacy and protection in AI systems
SCİS to conduct information-security risk analysis in AI application areas of government bodies
SCİS to take necessary measures ensuring AI-related information security in government bodies
Align national AI framework with international standards and best practices
Mitigate data-privacy risks through regulatory alignment and infrastructure investment
Implement continuous risk identification, assessment, and mitigation for AI systems
Ensure transparency and accountability in AI system deployment
Ensure human oversight and control over AI systems in public administration
Document sources or owners of datasets used
Describe how datasets further the intended purpose of the AI system
Document number of data points (general ranges, estimated for dynamic datasets)
Describe types of data points within datasets
Document copyright, trademark, or patent status of datasets
Document whether datasets were purchased or licensed
Document whether datasets include personal information (CCPA § 1798.140(v))
Document whether datasets include aggregate consumer information (CCPA § 1798.140(b))
Document data cleaning, processing, or other modification (including intended purpose)
Document time period during which data was collected (including ongoing notice)
Document dates datasets were first used during development
Document whether datasets were/will be distributed or posted, and terms
Estimation methods allowed when information not reasonably available — describe methods and explain gaps
Write, implement, comply with, and publish frontier AI framework
Incorporate national, international, and industry-consensus standards
Define and assess catastrophic-risk thresholds (may include multiple-tiered thresholds)
Apply mitigations based on assessment results
Review assessments and mitigation adequacy before deployment
Define criteria for framework updates and substantial modification disclosures
Describe cybersecurity practices to secure frontier models
Describe approach to identifying and responding to critical safety incidents
Describe governance practices to ensure framework implementation
Assess and manage catastrophic risk from internal use (including oversight circumvention)
Annual review and update of frontier AI framework
Material modification publication within 30 days with justification
Transparency report must contain capabilities, safety testing, and mitigations information
Report critical safety incidents to OES within 15 days of discovery
Report imminent-risk incidents (death/serious bodily injury) within 24 hours
Provide anonymous internal disclosure process for covered employees
Monthly update to whistleblowers on investigation status and actions taken
Detection tool must assess whether image, video, or audio was created/altered by provider's GenAI
Offer user option to include manifest disclosure in AI-generated content
Manifest disclosure identifies content as AI-generated, clear and conspicuous
Include latent disclosure in AI-generated image, video, or audio content
Latent disclosure conveys provider name, system name/version, timestamp, unique identifier
Latent disclosure detectable by provider's detection tool
ADMT definition — technology that processes personal information and uses computation to replace/substantially replace human decisionmaking
Human involvement criteria — reviewer must know how to interpret output, review it, and have authority to make/change decision
Profiling inclusion — ADMT includes profiling that replaces/substantially replaces human decisionmaking
Provide Pre-use Notice before using ADMT for significant decisions
Plain language explanation of specific purpose for ADMT use
Describe consumer's right to opt-out of ADMT and how to submit request
Inform consumer of human appeal option when opt-out exception applies
Identify specific exception relied upon when opt-out not required
Describe consumer's right to access ADMT and how to submit request
State non-retaliation prohibition for exercising CCPA rights
Include additional information about how ADMT works for significant decisions
Provide ability to opt-out of ADMT for significant decisions
Methods must be easy to use, no dark patterns
Collect only personal information necessary for opt-out processing
Confirm receipt of opt-out request within 10 business days
Honor opt-out within 15 business days
Honor opt-out for at least 12 months
Allow consumer to renew opt-out request
Provide information about ADMT use when responding to access requests
Plain language explanation of specific purpose for ADMT use
Plain language explanation of role ADMT plays in decisionmaking process
Non-retaliation notice and instructions for exercising other CCPA rights
Easy-to-use request methods, no dark patterns
Comply with verification requirements for access requests
Inform requestor if identity cannot be verified
Explain basis for denial if request conflicts with law or CCPA exception
Disclose non-denied information for partial denials
Use reasonable security measures when transmitting requested information
Conduct risk assessments for processing presenting significant privacy/security risk
Perform annual cybersecurity audits
Identify and document categories of sensitive personal information
Weigh benefits against risks with goal of restricting processing if risks outweigh benefits
Create risk assessment report with specified information
Provide Notice at Collection for personal information collection
Identify business purposes for collecting personal information
Identify categories of personal information to be collected
Disclose consumer rights (know, delete, correct, opt-out, limit, ADMT opt-out, ADMT access)
State non-retaliation right for exercising privacy rights
Include instructions for exercising CCPA rights and process expectations
Right to know about personal information collected, including AI-generated inferences
Right to delete personal information including AI-generated inferences
Right to correct inaccurate personal information including AI-generated inferences
Right to opt-out of sale or sharing including AI-generated profiles
Right to limit use of sensitive personal information in AI systems
Prohibit subliminal manipulation techniques that distort human behavior causing harm
Prohibit generic social scoring systems evaluating persons based on social behavior
Prohibit biometric categorization based on sensitive personal data
Prohibit real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces (with judicial-authorization exceptions)
Prohibit non-selective facial-image extraction from the internet or CCTV
Prohibit emotion recognition in workplaces and educational institutions
Establish continuous, iterative risk-management system across the AI lifecycle
Implement data governance with bias detection and representativeness assessment
Maintain technical documentation demonstrating compliance
Implement automatic event-logging (registros) for traceability across lifecycle
Ensure transparency and explainability sufficient for operators and affected persons to understand system operation
Design for effective human oversight — system must be controllable and monitorable by natural persons
Guarantee adequate levels of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity per Ley N° 21.663
Ensure system respects fundamental rights and prevents stereotyping or degradation of persons or groups
Establish and document post-implementation monitoring system proportional to identified risks
Adopt immediate corrective measures — deactivate, withdraw from market, or suspend the system
Mark synthetic content (audio, image, video, text) in machine-readable format detectable as AI-generated or manipulated
Ensure technical marking solutions are effective, interoperable, and proportionate to content type and state of the art
Inform persons clearly and timely when interacting with limited-risk AI systems (except when evident from context)
Ensure limited-risk systems provide transparency, explainability, and security proportional to risk level
Observe general principles: human oversight, safety, transparency, accountability, fairness, environmental well-being
Maintain risk registers and documentation for audit and conformity assessment
Generate content complying with laws, regulations, and social morality
Prevent generation of prohibited content (national security, ethnic unity, terrorism, obscenity, false information, IP violations)
Generate high-quality, accurate content
Respect intellectual property and commercial secrets in training data and generated content
Protect personal information and data security
Use lawful data sources and foundation models for training
Do not infringe IP rights in training data
Obtain individual consent or lawful basis for personal information in training data (PIPL)
Take measures to improve training data quality (authenticity, accuracy, objectivity, diversity)
Take remedial measures against unlawful content in training data
Formulate clear, specific, operational annotation rules compliant with the Measures
Conduct data annotation quality assessment and sample-check accuracy
Bear network information security responsibilities as content producer
Bear personal information processor responsibilities under PIPL
Clearly specify and publicly disclose intended user groups, contexts, and purposes
Protect users' input information and usage records
Do not collect non-essential personal information
Do not illegally retain input information that can identify users
Do not illegally provide user input information to others
Timely accept and handle user requests for access, copy, amend, supplement, or delete personal information
Mark images, videos, and other generated content per Deep Synthesis Provisions (2022)
Prominent identification for AI dialogue, synthetic voice, face generation, immersive scenes
Include prominent identification function for other deep synthesis services
Provide safe, stable, and continuous services
Detect illegal content generated by the service
Immediately cease generation and transmission when illegal content found
Eliminate illegal content when detected
Report illegal content incidents to competent authorities
Take measures against users misusing services (warnings, restrictions, suspension, termination)
Save records of user misuse incidents
Report user misuse to relevant authorities
Establish complaint and reporting mechanisms
Timely accept, process, and provide feedback on complaints
Conduct security assessment for services with public opinion or social mobilization attributes
Provide explanations of training data sources, scale, types, annotation rules, algorithm mechanisms
Duty of reasonable care to avoid algorithmic discrimination
Provide deployers with statement disclosing intended uses, known/foreseeable limitations, performance evaluations, data used to train
Disclose to deployers known/foreseeable algorithmic discrimination risks
Make available documentation needed to complete impact assessment
Disclose to attorney general + known deployers within 90 days when discovers high-risk AI system has caused or is reasonably likely to cause algorithmic discrimination
Duty of reasonable care to avoid algorithmic discrimination
Implement risk-management policy + program — iterative, identify+document+mitigate known/foreseeable risks
Complete impact assessment annually + within 90 days after intentional + substantial modification
Specific impact-assessment contents — purpose, intended use cases, deployment context, benefits, analysis of risks, transparency measures, post-deployment monitoring
Notify consumer of high-risk AI system use before/at time of consequential decision
Provide consumer with statement disclosing — purpose, decision basis, principal factors, sources, right to opt out (where applicable)
Provide opportunity to correct incorrect personal data
Provide opportunity to appeal adverse consequential decision to human reviewer
Disclose to attorney general within 90 days of discovering high-risk AI system has caused algorithmic discrimination
AI system interacting with consumer shall disclose AI involvement (unless obvious)
Affirmative defense for developers + deployers using nationally/internationally recognized risk-management framework (e.g., NIST AI RMF)
Implement evidence-based methods to detect user expressions of suicide, self-harm, or imminent physical violence
Prevent AI companion from generating output encouraging self-harm, violence, or unlawful substance consumption
Disclose to users that they are interacting with AI (continuously visible or at start of 24-hour period + hourly for minors / every 3 hours for adults)
Prevent AI companion from claiming to be human or generating output refuting non-human identity
For minors: prevent manipulative techniques (emotional dependence, isolation, romantic interactions, engagement optimization) and provide parental tools for screen time and account settings
Large frontier developers must investigate reports, take immediate action to eliminate danger, and provide reasonable updates to reporting employees
Developers must provide deployers all information necessary to comply with AEDT disclosure obligations (or contractually assume them)
Deployers must disclose to employees/applicants in plain language that they are interacting with an AEDT
Deployers must provide written pre-decision notice: AEDT use, purpose, nature of decision, trade name, data categories/sources, data assessment methods, deployer contact info
Deployers must provide additional notice for adverse decisions (right to request human review)
Courts and CHRO may consider evidence of anti-bias testing and proactive efforts when adjudicating discrimination claims

Employers must not use AEDT in a manner that causes discrimination based on protected characteristics (race, color, creed, age, sex, gender identity, marital status, national origin, ancestry, disability, veteran status)
Covered providers (>1M monthly users, publicly accessible generative AI) must include provenance data in audio, image, or video content created or materially altered by their AI
Provenance data must be difficult to tamper with, remove, or disassociate using commercially and technically reasonable methods (e.g., C2PA standard)
Provenance data must allow consumers to assess whether content was created or materially altered by generative AI
AI subscription providers must disclose quantitative/qualitative limitations, usage caps, feature restrictions, and provider discretion to reduce quality/functionality (Sec. 1, effective Oct 1, 2026)
AI subscription providers must disclose modifications to limitations upon renewal
State agencies must inventory AI systems in use (Sec. 37, effective Oct 1, 2026)
Covered platforms must not deliver personalized algorithmic content recommendations to minors without verifiable parental consent
Default settings for minors: 1-hour daily algorithmic-content limit, private account mode, notification curfew (8 a.m.–9 p.m.), sensitive-content blocking
Registration and retention of information collected during surveillance activities (§ 5(2))
Temporary prohibitions on AI systems using Art. 5 prohibited practices (§ 7)
Mandatory compliance for systems handling confidential, restricted, or personal data (Art. 17)
Algorithmic Impact Assessment for Consequential Decision systems before deployment (Impl. Decree Art. 15)
Identify, assess, and mitigate risks for high-risk AI systems within ANIA framework
Regulatory sandbox / testing environment for experimental AI activities (Art. 4(f), 19(b))
Inform users whether a decision was made directly by AI or driven by AI for commercial or rights/services access (Art. 18)
Provide understandable and transparent explanations of the decision-making process (Art. 18)
Establish mechanisms to challenge AI decisions before a competent natural person who may confirm, modify, or revoke (Art. 18)
No liability for research/experimental/sandbox activities not commercially deployed and not interfering with user rights (Art. 19(b))
All lifecycle stakeholders — developers, implementers, service providers, end-users — responsible for their respective roles (Art. 20)
Developers must design AI systems complying with ethical and technical standards; implementers ensure proper implementation and security (Art. 20)
Respect fairness, transparency, accountability, informed consent, data minimization, inclusion, and non-discrimination (Art. 5)
Address risks of biases, misuse, and misinformation in AI systems (Art. 2(b))
Personal data use in AI must comply with the Law for the Protection of Personal Data (Art. 22)
Public-sector AI must comply with ACE technical security regulations in addition to ANIA criteria (Art. 16)

Compliance with EU AI Act — all provisions interpreted in conformity with Regulation (EU) 2024/1689
Anthropocentric approach — human autonomy and decision-making power
Fundamental rights compliance
Core principles — transparency, proportionality, security, data protection, accuracy, non-discrimination, sustainability
Data and process quality — correctness, reliability, security, quality, appropriateness, transparency
Human oversight and intervention
Cybersecurity as essential precondition — resilience against alteration attempts
Lawful, fair, transparent data processing compatible with collection purposes
Clear and simple information about data processing and AI use
Parental consent for under-14s accessing AI technologies
Autonomous consent for 14-18 year olds with accessible information
AI must improve healthcare, prevention, diagnosis, treatment respecting rights
Non-discrimination in healthcare access
Patient right to be informed about AI use
Medical decision authority remains with professionals — AI is support only
System reliability, periodic verification, and updates to minimize errors
AI must improve working conditions and protect worker psychophysical integrity
Workplace AI must be safe, reliable, transparent, non-violating of dignity
Employer must inform worker of AI use per remote-worker monitoring rules
Non-discrimination in employment AI (sex, age, ethnicity, religion, sexual orientation, political opinions)
AI use in intellectual professions is ancillary and supportive only
Professional must inform client of AI use in clear, simple, exhaustive language
PA must use AI to increase efficiency, reduce time, improve service quality
PA must ensure knowability of AI functioning and traceability of use
AI use is instrumental and supportive — decision authority with human
Magistrate decision authority — all interpretation, fact assessment, measures reserved to magistrate
AI for sports organization in compliance with general principles
Voluntary adoption of AI governance practices per government guidelines
Voluntary risk management for AI systems
Voluntary human oversight of AI systems
Voluntary transparency and disclosure
Voluntary security measures for AI systems
Art. 6: AI systems must ensure fairness and equality, excluding discrimination (origin, gender, race, nationality, language, religion, etc.)
Art. 7: Provide users complete information about AI system characteristics and limitations; right to be informed about automated processing and right to object
Art. 8(1): Owner/possessor/user must ensure continuous control over the AI system at all lifecycle stages
Art. 9: Preserve human autonomy and free will in decision-making when operating AI systems

Art. 10(1)–(2): AI use subject to data-protection requirements; no unauthorized collection/storage/dissemination of personal data; prevent unauthorized third-party access; use high-quality representative data sets
Art. 11(1): AI systems must meet safety and reliability requirements that exclude unforeseen consequences or abuse
Art. 11(2): Results of AI system activities must comply with the legislation of the Republic of Kazakhstan
Art. 15(2)(1) + Art. 11(3): Owners/holders must implement risk management of AI systems
Art. 18(1): Continuous risk management — identify, assess, mitigate risks; update at least annually
Art. 18(2): If prohibited capabilities (Art. 17(3)) are identified, take immediate measures including suspend or terminate operation
Art. 15(2)(2)–(3): Ensure security and reliability including protection from unauthorized access and failures; maintain documentation based on impact degree
Art. 16(1)(4): Right to receive explanations about AI results affecting rights, freedoms, and legitimate interests
Art. 16(1)(5): Right to request information about the data on which AI decisions were based
Art. 16(2)(1)–(2): Users must use AI only within granted access rights and comply with safety rules
Art. 17(1): Classify AI systems by risk level (minimal, medium, high) based on impact on users, society, and state
Art. 17(2): Classify AI systems by autonomy degree (low, medium, high) based on independence in decision-making
Art. 17(3)(1)–(2): Prohibited — subconscious/manipulative techniques distorting behavior; exploitation of vulnerabilities (age, disability, social status)
Art. 17(3)(3): Prohibited — social scoring: assessment/classification of individuals based on social behavior or personal characteristics
Art. 17(3)(4)–(5): Prohibited — unlawful personal-data collection/processing; biometric classification for discrimination (race, political views, religion)
Art. 17(3)(6)–(7): Prohibited — emotion determination without consent; creation/distribution of legally prohibited AI results
Art. 19(2) + Art. 20(2): Audit of AI systems for trusted-list inclusion — assess data-library quality/legitimacy and presence of prohibited capabilities
Art. 21(1): Users must be informed that goods, works, and services are produced or provided using AI systems
Art. 21(2)–(3): Synthetic AI results (deepfakes) must be marked in machine-readable form with visual/audio warning; owner/holder responsible
Art. 22(1)–(2): Machine-readable forms must be used for transparency and accountability, enabling automated recognition of conditions by AI systems
Art. 27(2)(5) + Art. 27(2): Data-library producer info must be in machine-readable form at creation and transfer; ensure data quality and compliance
Design AI systems to avoid bias and discrimination, including unintentional biases in data processing
Identify potential sources of bias in training data and take steps to mitigate them (developer best practice — Data Collection)
AI systems should operate reliably and consistently under normal and unexpected conditions
Implement fail-safe mechanisms to prevent harm in critical situations
Resist being compromised by unauthorised parties
Conduct rigorous testing and validation to ensure AI performs as accurately as intended (developer best practice — Validation)
Implement automatic measures to prevent data privacy breaches (rigorous testing, monitoring, fail-safe mechanisms)
Ensure data security and minimise potential harm, especially in high-risk areas (military, healthcare)
Incorporate user feedback to improve AI results and embed in applications (developer best practice — User Feedback)
(a) Full disclosure that an AI system is being used in decision-making
(b) Disclose the intended purpose of the AI system
(c) Provide information on training data and potential historical or social biases
(e) Provide mechanisms for challenging AI decisions
Provide clear explanations and justifications for AI decisions (developer best practice — Transparency)
Developers, owners, and operators accountable for system performance and compliance with governance and ethical principles
Address system purpose to avoid consequential harm (Accountability element 1)
Address technology capability to avoid consequential harm (Accountability element 2)
Address quality and reliability to avoid consequential harm (Accountability element 3)
Design AI to enhance societal values and prioritize human-centred values for betterment of individuals and communities
(a) Human-in-the-loop (HITL) — capability for human intervention in every decision cycle
(b) Human-on-the-loop (HOTL) — human intervention during design cycle and monitoring of system operation
(c) Human-in-command (HIC) — oversight of overall AI activity and ability to decide when and how to use the system
Prevent harm or misuse of AI technology
Incorporate ethical considerations into AI design and proactively identify/address ethical challenges (Ethical Considerations)
Continuously monitor AI performance and impact to identify biases, risks, or unintended consequences (Ongoing Monitoring)
End-user right to be informed about data collection
Conformity assessment procedures for high-risk AI systems prior to market placement
Providers and importers maintain technical files and make them available to MDIA on request
Implement risk management system for high-risk AI systems (identify, assess, mitigate)
Maintain technical documentation demonstrating conformity (Annex IV)
Automatic event logging for high-risk AI systems operating with recorded logs
Record-keeping of risk-management iterations and design decisions
Transparency obligations — notify users when interacting with AI (limited-transparency systems)
Label AI-generated content (deepfakes, synthetic media) distinguishable from non-AI content
Human oversight of high-risk AI systems, including named responsible person
Operational limits on automated decision-making with human-in-the-loop review
Providers implement post-market monitoring system for high-risk AI

Audit must include: selection rates per category, impact ratios per category, number of individuals assessed
Audit summary must include date of audit, source + explanation of data used, categories assessed
Notice to candidate residing in NYC at least 10 business days before AEDT use — that AEDT will be used + job qualifications + characteristics AEDT uses
Allow candidate to request alternative selection process or accommodation
Information about data type + source + retention policy

Tripartite risk classification of AI systems: prohibited (uso indebido), high-risk (riesgo alto), acceptable (riesgo aceptable)
Prohibited: deceptive or manipulative influence on human decision-making using subliminal techniques or exploitation of vulnerabilities
Prohibited: lethal autonomous systems that make decisions without human supervision causing physical harm
Prohibited: inference of sensitive attributes (race, politics, religion, sexual orientation) from biometric data
Prohibited: crime prediction based on personality profiling or trait assessment
High-risk classification: critical infrastructure, education, employment, social programs, credit, healthcare, emotion inference
Mandatory impact assessment for public-entity high-risk AI before development or implementation (Art. 30)
Voluntary impact assessment for private-sector high-risk AI (Art. 32)
Mitigation measures when risks detected: model adjustments, data-quality improvement, human-oversight mechanisms
Documentation retention for private impact assessments: minimum 3 years, available to judicial/administrative authorities
Prior, clear, and simple notification to users of AI system purpose, functionality, and decision types
Visible AI labeling of products, services, or AI-generated content, including capabilities and limitations
Explanation of automated decisions affecting human rights, in accessible language with key criteria and factors
Privacy by design: data minimization, anonymization techniques integrated into AI system development (Art. 29.b)
Bias identification and minimization using best practices, national/international standards
Risk management: encryption, anomaly detection, model robustness measures for AI systems
Security audits: mandatory before deployment and periodic during operation, identifying vulnerabilities and biases
Incident management: AI-related incidents managed as part of Information Security Management System
Human oversight of high-risk AI in public entities: health, education, justice, finance, social services (Art. 28.11)
Personnel must have capacity to stop, correct, or invalidate AI decisions (Art. 28.11.ii, 31.4.ii)
Updated, accessible registry of system operation principles, data sources, algorithm logic, and social/ethical impacts (Art. 31.1)
Policies, protocols, and procedures for security, privacy, transparency, explainability, and accountability (Art. 31.2)

Generative AI output labeling when outputs may be difficult to distinguish from non-AI content
Realistic content (deepfake) labeling with clear AI-generation indicators
Implement safety measures for compute-threshold systems (≥10²⁶ FLOPs)
Establish risk management system to monitor and respond to AI safety accidents
Identify, assess, and mitigate risks for compute-threshold systems
Conduct prior review to determine whether AI constitutes high-impact AI
Formulate and operate risk management plan for high-impact AI
Formulate and implement explanation plan (results, criteria, training data overview)
Formulate and operate user protection plan for high-impact AI
Assign human management and oversight of high-impact AI, including named contact person

Human autonomy — support human autonomy, respect personality rights and fundamental rights, ensure human oversight
Privacy protection and data governance — protect personal data, respect trade secrets, data minimization, promote open reuse of non-sensitive data
Cybersecurity and safety — establish cybersecurity measures, prevent threats, ensure robustness and safety
Transparency and explainability — AI outputs accompanied by appropriate disclosures or labeling to facilitate risk assessment
Accountability — ensure internal governance responsibilities and external social responsibilities are assumed
Prevent AI from infringing upon people's life, body, liberty, or property (Art. 5 ¶1)
High-risk AI products must carry advisory notices or warnings (Art. 5 ¶2)
MODA to promote internationally interoperable AI risk taxonomy and assessment framework (Art. 16 ¶1)
AI outputs shall be accompanied by appropriate disclosures or labeling
Disclosures shall facilitate assessment of potential risks and understanding of impact on rights and interests
Establish cybersecurity measures to prevent security threats and attacks
Ensure robustness and safety of the system
Protect personal data privacy; respect corporate trade secrets; avoid data leakage (Art. 4(3))
Adopt data minimization principle (Art. 4(3))
Avoid unnecessary personal data collection/processing; privacy by design and by default (Art. 14)
Support human autonomy; ensure human oversight of AI systems (Art. 4(2))
Government shall conduct risk assessments when using AI to perform duties or services (Art. 19 ¶1)
Government shall plan risk response measures (Art. 19 ¶1)
Government shall establish usage guidelines or internal control management mechanisms (Art. 19 ¶2)
Ensure internal governance responsibilities and external social responsibilities are assumed (Art. 4(7))
Prohibition on AI manipulation to incite self-harm
Prohibition on AI manipulation to harm others
Prohibition on AI manipulation to encourage criminal activity
Prohibition on government social scoring systems
Prohibition on government biometric data capture without consent
Prohibition on AI designed to infringe constitutional rights
Prohibition on AI designed for unlawful discrimination
Prohibition on AI for child pornography production/distribution
Prohibition on AI simulating child sexual conduct
Government agency AI disclosure to consumers before/at interaction
Disclosure must be clear, conspicuous, plain language, no dark patterns
Healthcare AI disclosure timing — by date service first provided (emergency: ASAP)
Rebuttable presumption of reasonable care for NIST AI RMF compliance
Disclosure upon request — clearly and conspicuously disclose AI interaction when asked in consumer-protection-regulated activities
Regulated occupation disclosure — prominently disclose when interacting with generative AI in regulated services
Verbal disclosure timing — at start of oral exchange for regulated occupations
Electronic disclosure timing — before written exchange for regulated occupations

description: Frozen baseline for coverage-matrix ratchet. CI fails if validated_count drops below this value OR if any validated_rows identity is no longer passing. Raise by re-running --init-baseline after improving evidence coverage.
